text: The surgeon inserts a varus needle through the inferior crease of the umbilicus, preparing for laparoscopic access.
The surgeon inserts a telescope through the supraumbilical port, providing a laparoscopic view for the mini gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon places a left epigastric port for the insertion of a stapler, preparing to create a horizontal staple line.
The surgeon positions ports in the left hypochondrium, right hypochondrium, and left anterior axillary line for the mini gastric bypass procedure.

The video shows the placement of a standard port for the mini gastric bypass procedure, following the previous setup of various ports including the supraumbilical port for the telescope and the left epigastric port for the stapler.
The surgeon performs a suction test followed by a hanging drop test, lifting the abdominal wall. The varus needle is then attached, using a striker for the procedure.
The surgeon uses a 45-liter insufflator in bariatric mode to insufflate the abdomen, reaching a pressure of 15 mmHg. A supraumbilical port is utilized for the insertion of the telescope.
The surgeon introduces a 10mm port for the telescope, allowing inspection of the abdominal cavity. Insufflator tubing is attached, and the flow rate is increased to 20 liters per minute.
The surgeon observes minor adhesions of the omentum attached to the right hypochondrium near the gallbladder area.
The surgeon introduces the right hypochondriac port, positioning it in the upper right abdominal area for access during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon prepares the supraumbilical port for the introduction of a surgical stapler, which will be used in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon positions the left hypochondriac port, providing access to the surgical field for the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of working ports in the abdominal area, which are used for instrument access during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon uses the epigastric port to lift the omentum, providing better access and visibility to the surgical field.
The surgeon positions a lever retractor through the right hypochondriac port, preparing to manipulate surrounding tissues during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon uses the right hand to hold the ligature through the left hypochondriac port.
An instrument is used to lift the liver, providing better access and visibility to the surgical field.
The surgical team proceeds without using the Nathanson lever retractor, focusing on manipulating the gastric structures with alternative instruments.
The surgeon lifts the lesser omentum at the level of the second gastric pedicle and creates an omental window. The window is then enlarged to inspect for any adhesions below the stomach.
The surgeon uses a ligature to create a window in the omental tissue, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgical field shows minimal adhesion below the stomach, allowing for clear visualization of the area.
The surgeon uses a stapler through the epigastric port to create a horizontal line on the gastric tissue.
The surgeon prepares to use a stapler to create the gastric tube, a key step in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon prepares to fire the first cartridge of the stapler, positioning it to create a staple line on the gastric tissue.
The surgeon uses a stapler through the epigastric port to create a horizontal line on the gastric tissue, visible as a linear staple line.
The surgeon pushes the bougie slightly and introduces it into the newly formed gastric tube, ensuring proper alignment within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins placing staples along the vertical line of the gastric tube using a stapler inserted through the epigastric port.
The surgeon ensures there are no adhesions in the retrogastric area before applying the staple to create the gastric tube.
The surgeon separates any visible adhesions in the retrogastric area using either a harmonic scalpel or ligature device, ensuring a clear surgical field for staple application.
The surgeon continues the vertical staple line on the gastric tissue, periodically inspecting the area and performing adhesiolysis before progressing with additional staple firings.
After firing the second staple, the surgeon lifts the stomach using a grasper, providing a clear view of the surgical area.
The surgeon inspects the retrogastric area, confirming that there are no adhesions present, ensuring a clear view for the continuation of the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgical field shows minimal adhesion around the stomach area, allowing for clear visualization and easier manipulation during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon introduces the third cartridge into the stapler, preparing to continue the vertical staple line along the stomach.
The surgeon introduces the third staple cartridge into the surgical field, preparing to continue the stapling process on the gastric tissue.
The surgeon examines the retrogastric area, noting the presence of minor adhesions visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform adhesiolysis in the retrogastric area, carefully cutting through any remaining adhesions.
The surgeon introduces the third staple cartridge near the gastric pouch, positioning it for optimal advantage in the stapling process.
The surgeon measures the length of intestine to be bypassed, illustrating the malabsorptive component of the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon positions the stapler and waits for a few seconds before firing each staple, ensuring precise placement during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon lifts the gastric tissue to inspect the posterior and retro-gastric areas for any visible adhesions.
The surgeon uses a grasper to lift the tissue, allowing the telescope to enter the retro gastric area for visualization, checking for the presence of adhesions.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to perform adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions in the retro gastric area.
The surgeon inspects the anterior stomach area, noting potential adhesions between the spleen and the stomach, as well as adhesions in the retro-gastric fat cushion.
The surgeon uses a ligature or harmonic device to separate adhesions in the retro gastric area, performing blunt dissection to create a window and expose the diaphragm.
The surgeon mobilizes the petroleum over the left crus of the diaphragm, facilitating access to the retro gastric area.
The surgeon uses the left hand to lift the stomach while the right hand guides the telescope into the retrogastric tunnel, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon slowly performs blunt dissection, revealing the shiny fascia as they progress through the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the diaphragm after creating a window through the adhesions, allowing the stapler to pass beyond the gastric fundus. The diaphragm is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon visualizes the diaphragm through the created window, applies suction, and prepares to use a 60 mm cartridge on the stapler. The cartridge is positioned, and the stapler is fired to continue the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the cut staple line, showing the separation of tissue following the application of the stapler.
The surgeon has reached the fundus of the stomach during the gastric bypass procedure, preparing for further surgical manipulation.
A small portion of the fundus tag is visibly attached at the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon removes the previous staple cartridge and introduces a new one. A small tissue tag is visible and will be cut out with the new cartridge.

After completing the previous step, the surgeon performs suction irrigation to clear the area, confirming there is no bleeding. The omentum is lifted, and the small bowel is maneuvered to reach the first part of the jejunum near the ligament of Treitz, with visible fat deposits typical in morbidly obese patients.
The surgeon tilts the surgical table to facilitate pulling the intestinal loop, visually confirming its reach up to the ligamentum teres.
The surgeon visually identifies the transition point between the fourth part of the duodenum and the start of the jejunum, indicating that further pulling of the jejunum is not possible.
The surgeon visually identifies the first part of the jejunum, which is exposed and positioned for further surgical manipulation.
The ligamentum teres is clearly visible in the surgical field, indicating the anatomical location reached during the procedure.

The surgeon lifts the greater omentum to visualize the transverse colon, ensuring the identification of the ligamentum teres. The anatomical structures are clearly visible as the surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue.
The surgeon begins manipulating the jejunum, preparing to work on this section of the intestine.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the jejunum, ensuring there is no torsion as the segment is positioned.
The surgeon points to the stomach located just above the staple line, focusing on the middle of the staple line along the anterior wall.
A window is created in the stomach using a harmonic scalpel, perforating the stomach wall. The harmonic device is then inserted into the perforated area.
The surgeon inserts the gastric calibration tube into the stomach, positioning it carefully to ensure proper placement.
The lumen of the stomach is visible after creating a window and perforating the stomach wall.
The surgeon cuts a section of the jejunum, visibly separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon introduces an instrument to perforate the tissue, following the previous steps of cutting and positioning the jejunum.
The surgeon ensures that the mucosa is clearly visible after cutting the jejunum, avoiding the formation of a subserosal pocket.
The surgeon uses graspers to manipulate the tissue, preparing for further surgical steps in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon introduces a surgical stapler into the operative field, preparing to staple tissue as part of the gastric bypass procedure.
One jaw of the surgical instrument enters the jejunum, indicating the start of an anastomosis or tissue manipulation in the gastric bypass procedure.

The surgeon positions the face jaw of the cartridge against the stomach, preparing to perform a side-to-side staple using a 60mm stapler. Both ends of the cartridge are fully pulled to ensure proper alignment and engagement with the tissue.
The surgeon prepares to fire the 60mm side-to-side stapler, ensuring it is fully positioned on the stomach for the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon fires the stapler, creating an anastomosis between the gastric pouch and the small intestine.
The surgeon removes the surgical instrument from the anastomosis site, ensuring the blood supply is not compromised.
The surgeon completes the removal of a structure, which is now visibly outside the surgical field.
The surgeon places a suction device to lift the tissue, ensuring the anastomosis is intact and properly formed.
The buzi is visible within the lumen of the jejunum, providing a clear view of the internal structure.
The surgeon is closing the suture line, carefully stitching the tissue to ensure a secure closure.
The surgeon begins the closure of the suture line, ensuring the edges of the tissue are aligned and secured.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to create a perforation in the staple line, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a barb suture to close the suture line, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to close the suture line, ensuring the edges of the tissue are approximated securely.
The surgeon begins the first layer of a two-layer closure technique, using sutures to secure the tissue.
The surgeon approximates the serosal layer using a suturing technique, ensuring closure of the gastric tissue.
The surgeon begins tying a surgeon's knot using the suture material, demonstrating the technique for securing the suture in place.
The surgeon performs two opposite alternating wraps using a barb suture, securing the tissue layers in preparation for the next step of the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon continues to close the staple hole, using sutures to secure the tissue and ensure proper closure.
The surgeon visually identifies the jejunum and the stomach line, highlighting the anatomical structures involved in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon continues to close the staple hole in the gastric tissue, using sutures to secure the closure.
The surgeon uses a continuous suturing technique to join the jejunum to the stomach line, ensuring a secure connection between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses the left hand to separate the jejunum from the stomach line, maintaining clear visualization of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses the left instrument to gently lift the falciform ligament, providing better visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an edge-to-edge suturing technique, taking continuous bites along the tissue edge.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing along the anastomosis line, ensuring the suture does not occlude the lumen. A bougie may be introduced to guide the suturing process and prevent accidental lumen occlusion.
The surgeon works on the margins of the mucosa, ensuring the mucosa is inverted to prevent occlusion of the suture line during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon continues the suturing process, maintaining edge-to-edge alignment of the tissue while ensuring the lumen is not occluded.
The surgeon uses the left hand to support the suture while the right hand takes a bite, continuing the suturing process.
The surgeon continues suturing, ensuring that the stitches are placed closely together without excessive pulling to prevent cut-through of the tissue.
The surgeon reaches the corners of the anastomosis and interlocks the suture, ensuring it extends to the healthy margin of the tissue.
The surgeon focuses on closing the corners of the gastric bypass site using locking sutures, ensuring the edges are adequately secured to prevent any gaps.
The surgeon interlocks the suture at the corner of the tissue, ensuring secure closure during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon performs a locking suture technique, continuing the suturing process with precise movements to secure the tissue.
The surgeon extends the suture to the healthy margin, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the healthy margin of the gastric tissue and applies interlocking sutures to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon demonstrates the interlocking of sutures at the healthy margin of the gastric tissue, ensuring secure closure.
The surgeon continues suturing along the healthy margin, ensuring the tissue is securely interlocked.
The surgeon takes an additional bite with the suture, then returns to the previous position to perform a second layer of suturing, connecting the serosa to serosa.
The surgeon reinforces the first staple line, ensuring secure closure of the gastric tissue.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue, ensuring the integrity of the seromuscular layer is maintained during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the closure of the enteroenterostomy hole, ensuring the surgical site is fully sealed.
The surgeon uses the initial tail of the suture to terminate the stitch, performing a double wrap followed by a single wrap to secure the closure.
The surgeon removes the suture using scissors after closing the incision, then proceeds to check the lumen for patency.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the suture, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon catches the suture along with the waist tail and pulls it out, ensuring the integrity of the seromuscular layer is maintained.
The surgeon uses two graspers to hold either side of the lumen, stabilizing the tissue for further manipulation or inspection.
The surgeon uses a long jaw clasp to occlude the lumen, while the anesthetist introduces methylene blue dye into the area.
The surgeon observes the lumen distended with methylene blue dye, indicating successful introduction and expansion within the gastric bypass site.
The distended gastric pouch is visible, with methylene blue dye flowing on either side, confirming the absence of any leaks.
The surgeon observes the distension of the lumen, noting the absence of blue color, indicating no leakage of methylene blue dye.
The surgeon inspects the entire staple line for any signs of leakage or irregularities, ensuring the integrity of the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon examines the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring proper visualization and assessment of the area.
The surgical field shows no visible blue color, indicating the absence of methylene blue leakage.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical site, confirming the absence of any leaks at the gastroesophageal junction and stomach.
The surgeon places a suture on the Shirodkar's line, securing the tissue in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon fixes both the efferent and afferent limbs of the gastric bypass, ensuring proper alignment and stability.
The surgeon fixes the afferent limb to the stomach part, specifically the gastric tube, ensuring proper alignment and attachment.
The surgeon uses a white reel to fix the structure to the anchoring suture, ensuring it is tension-free and securely tightened without tearing.
The surgeon ties an additional knot to secure the suture, ensuring it is tension-free and properly tightened.
The surgeon ties a knot to secure a loop around a portion of the stomach, completing the fixation of the loop.
The surgeon pulls out the suture and forms a reverse C shape to tighten the knot.
The surgeon releases tension on the jejunum over the staple line, ensuring the tissue is relaxed and properly aligned.
The surgeon pulls on the omentum, adjusting its position within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the final C-shaped suture and pulls the omentum to cover the entire anastomotic line.
The surgeon fixes the omentum to the interim part of the stomach, optionally securing it with a titanium clip or tying a knot, to cover the entire mini gastric bypass anastomosis.
